_doc.description: Strict seccomp allowlist for the BEAP certifier container (P3.3). Derived from sealer.json (P1.7) with the same syscall surface.
_doc.design: deny-by-default (SCMP_ACT_ERRNO); allowlist exactly the syscalls needed for a Node.js 20 HTTP server that performs Ed25519 signing over canonical certificate bytes. Target threat model: certifier never executes subprocesses, never writes to disk, never makes outbound network connections; holds EDGE_PRIVATE_KEY_HEX in memory only.
_doc.differences_from_sealer_json: None in syscall allowlist — certifier and sealer share the same Node.js runtime requirements. The certifier profile is a separate file (beap-certifier.json) so pod manifests can reference it independently and tighten or diverge later without affecting LOCAL_HOST sealer deployments.
removed_from_oci_runtime_default: execve / execveat      — sealer must never spawn subprocesses; this removes the most common code-execution escalation path
fork / vfork           — no subprocess spawning; Node.js uses clone(2) for threads, never fork
ptrace                 — no process introspection or debugging attach
process_vm_readv/writev — no cross-process memory access
keyctl / add_key / request_key — sealer manages its key in memory; no kernel keyring needed
bpf                    — no kernel eBPF programs
perf_event_open        — no performance counter attachment
personality            — no execution domain switching
modify_ldt / ioperm / iopl — no x86 hardware-level access
mount / umount / umount2 / pivot_root / chroot — no filesystem namespace ops
unshare / setns / clone with CLONE_NEW* flags — no new namespace creation
fanotify_init / inotify_init* — no filesystem event watchers
unlink / unlinkat / rmdir / rename / renameat* — no filesystem deletes (belt-and-suspenders: readOnlyRootFilesystem already blocks writes)
mkdir / mkdirat / mknod / mknodat — no directory/device creation
chmod / fchmod / fchmodat / chown / fchown / fchownat / lchown — no permission changes
truncate / ftruncate   — no file truncation
link / linkat / symlink / symlinkat — no hardlink/symlink creation
open (legacy)          — use openat instead (always relative-path-safe)
creat                  — no file creation
swapon / swapoff       — no swap management
syslog                 — no kernel log access
acct                   — no process accounting
quotactl               — no disk quota ops
setuid / setgid / setresuid / setresgid / setgroups — sealer runs at a fixed UID; no privilege changes
capset / capget        — capabilities already dropped; no further manipulation
nfsservctl             — NFS kernel server ops not needed
lookup_dcookie / uselib / pciconfig_* — legacy / hardware ops not needed
_doc.p1_7_note: The ultimate goal stated in Strategy §1.3 is 'read/write/futex/exit only'. Node.js 20 requires a broader set than those 4 syscalls to start its V8 runtime, libuv thread pool, and TLS stack. This profile represents the tightest practical allowlist for the role as implemented. Future work: compile sealer to a standalone binary (e.g. with Deno compile or Node SEA) to eliminate V8/libuv overhead and reduce the syscall surface further.

syscalls.comment: Core I/O — reads and writes to already-open file descriptors (sockets, pipes, stdout). NO filesystem-creating or filesystem-modifying calls here.
File descriptor duplication and seeking — needed by Node.js stdio and libuv.
File metadata (stat family) — needed by Node.js module loader at startup to stat .js files. After startup these are rarely called.
File open (read-only path) — openat is preferred over legacy open; sealer never creates files. Used by Node.js module loader at startup.
Directory iteration — needed by Node.js module resolution at startup.
Symbolic-link and path resolution — needed by Node.js module resolution.
Memory management — V8 JIT uses mmap/mprotect heavily; brk for heap; memfd_create for V8 shared memory. NO mlock (sealer doesn't need memory pinning).
Process identity — getpid/gettid used by Node.js logging and libuv; getuid/getgid used by Node.js permission checks. NO setuid/setgid/capset — UID is fixed.
prctl — needed by libuv for thread naming (PR_SET_NAME). Does NOT include PR_SET_SECCOMP (already applied by the container runtime).
Process exit — only clean-exit calls allowed. NO fork/vfork/execve/execveat.
Time and clock — needed by Node.js event loop timing, Date.now(), HMAC timestamp logging.
Signals — needed by Node.js process signal handling (SIGTERM for graceful shutdown) and libuv signal pipe. NO kill of other processes (tgkill is own-thread only in practice).
Synchronization primitives — futex is the core of pthreads (libuv thread pool). set_robust_list registers list of futexes to release on abnormal exit.
Thread creation — Node.js libuv uses pthreads (implemented via clone). NO clone with CLONE_NEWNS or other namespace flags (container runtime handles that).

Network I/O — certifier accepts inbound connections on loopback (127.0.0.1:18104) ONLY. NO outbound connect() calls are made by certifier.ts; egress deny is enforced by network policy and by role code (P3.4).
Polling / event notification — needed by Node.js event loop (libuv uses epoll on Linux).
IPC primitives — pipe2 for Node.js stdio pipes; eventfd2 and timerfd for libuv timers.
Randomness — getrandom is used by node:crypto and @noble/curves for Ed25519 signing. Security-critical for the certifier.
Miscellaneous kernel queries — uname and sysinfo used by Node.js at startup; fcntl for FD flags (O_NONBLOCK); ioctl for terminal probing.
Resource limits — getrlimit used by Node.js at startup to check file descriptor limits.
wait/waitpid — needed if Node.js ever spawns a child (it shouldn't for sealer, but libuv registers the handler). The actual fork/exec is blocked, making this a no-op in practice.
